sections.plain_summary: Thyroid dysfunction occurs when the thyroid gland — a small butterfly-shaped gland in the front of the neck — produces too much or too little thyroid hormone. These hormones regulate nearly every cell in the body, so when they are out of balance, the effects are wide-ranging: energy, metabolism, mood, digestion, temperature regulation, heart rate, and fertility can all be affected. Many people with thyroid dysfunction go undiagnosed for years, or receive a diagnosis but continue to feel unwell despite standard treatment.
sections.detailed_summary:
The thyroid gland produces two main hormones — thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3) — under the direction of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) released by the pituitary gland. T4 is the storage form and must be converted to the more active T3, primarily in the liver, gut, and peripheral tissues. This conversion step is a common point of failure that standard TSH-only testing often misses.

Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid) is the most common form, affecting women far more often than men. The most frequent cause is Hashimoto's thyroiditis, an autoimmune condition in which the immune system attacks thyroid tissue. Over time, this inflammatory damage reduces the gland's ability to produce adequate hormones. Symptoms tend to develop gradually and are often attributed to stress, aging, or depression — which frequently delays diagnosis by years.

Hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid) involves excess hormone production, most commonly caused by Graves' disease (another autoimmune condition) or toxic nodular goitre. Symptoms are often more acute and include palpitations, anxiety, weight loss, and heat intolerance.

Subclinical thyroid dysfunction — where TSH is abnormal but T3/T4 remain technically within range — is increasingly recognised as clinically significant, particularly when accompanied by symptoms and positive thyroid antibodies. Many people with subclinical hypothyroidism report the same symptom burden as those with overt hypothyroidism, yet frequently go untreated under conventional management protocols.

A critical but underappreciated issue is poor T4-to-T3 conversion. Some individuals have adequate circulating T4 but insufficient active T3, due to genetic variants (notably in the DIO2 gene), chronic inflammation, gut dysfunction, nutritional deficiencies, or high physiological stress. Standard levothyroxine (T4-only) treatment may therefore not fully resolve symptoms for these individuals. Combination T4/T3 therapy or natural desiccated thyroid (NDT) preparations may be more effective for this subgroup, though this remains contested in mainstream endocrinology.

prevalence.summary: Thyroid dysfunction is one of the most common endocrine disorders worldwide, with hypothyroidism being significantly more prevalent than hyperthyroidism.
prevalence.estimate: Hypothyroidism affects approximately 5% of the population; subclinical hypothyroidism affects a further 5–10%. Hyperthyroidism affects roughly 1%. Hashimoto's thyroiditis is estimated to affect 1–2% of the general population but may be higher in women of reproductive age.
prevalence.notes: Prevalence figures vary widely by population, iodine intake levels, and diagnostic criteria used. Women are 5–10 times more likely to be affected than men. Autoimmune thyroid disease peaks in the 30–50 age range. Many cases of subclinical dysfunction remain undetected. Some researchers argue that the current TSH reference range is set too broadly and that many people with 'normal' results are functionally hypothyroid.

subgroups.name: Hashimoto's thyroiditis (autoimmune hypothyroidism)
subgroups.description: The most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient countries. The immune system produces antibodies (anti-TPO and/or anti-thyroglobulin) that attack thyroid tissue, causing progressive loss of gland function. Symptoms may fluctuate, especially early in the disease, as damaged cells release stored hormone in bursts.
distinguishing_features: Positive anti-TPO and/or anti-thyroglobulin antibodies
Ultrasound may show heterogeneous, hypoechoic thyroid tissue
Symptoms may include Hashimoto's encephalopathy (rare but severe cognitive effects)
Periods of transient hyperthyroidism ('Hashitoxicosis') can precede overt hypothyroidism
Strong association with other autoimmune conditions (coeliac disease, Type 1 diabetes, lupus)
Gluten and other dietary triggers may worsen antibody levels in some individuals

subgroups.name: Graves' disease (autoimmune hyperthyroidism)
subgroups.description: An autoimmune condition in which antibodies (TSI/TRAb) stimulate the TSH receptor, causing the thyroid to overproduce hormones. The most common cause of hyperthyroidism in developed countries.
distinguishing_features: Positive TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb/TSI)
Exophthalmos (eye protrusion) or other ophthalmopathy in some cases
Pretibial myxoedema (skin thickening on lower legs) in a minority
Diffuse goitre on ultrasound
Rapid onset of palpitations, anxiety, heat intolerance, weight loss
Can go into remission spontaneously or after treatment

subgroups.name: Subclinical hypothyroidism
subgroups.description: TSH is elevated above the reference range but free T4 remains within normal limits. Often dismissed as not requiring treatment, despite many affected individuals experiencing significant symptoms.
distinguishing_features: TSH typically 2.5–10 mIU/L with normal free T4
May progress to overt hypothyroidism, especially when antibodies are present
Cardiovascular risk may be elevated, particularly with TSH above 7–10
Many clinicians will not treat unless TSH exceeds 10 — leaving symptomatic individuals without support
Symptoms often indistinguishable from overt hypothyroidism

subgroups.name: Poor T4-to-T3 converters
subgroups.description: Individuals who produce or take adequate T4 but fail to convert it efficiently to active T3, leaving cells functionally thyroid-deficient despite 'normal' standard blood tests.
distinguishing_features: Normal or high T4, low-normal or low free T3
Elevated reverse T3 (rT3) in some cases
Genetic variants in deiodinase enzymes (e.g. DIO2 polymorphisms)
Persistent symptoms on levothyroxine despite 'normal' TSH
May improve with addition of T3 (liothyronine) or switch to desiccated thyroid extract
Associated with chronic stress, gut dysfunction, selenium deficiency, and inflammation

subgroups.name: Postpartum thyroiditis
subgroups.description: Transient thyroid dysfunction occurring in the first 12 months after delivery, affecting approximately 5–10% of women. Often missed or attributed to postnatal depression or exhaustion.
distinguishing_features: Typically follows a pattern of hyperthyroidism then hypothyroidism
Usually resolves within 12–18 months but 20–30% develop permanent hypothyroidism
Anti-TPO antibodies often positive
Symptoms closely mimic postnatal depression and anxiety
Breastfeeding and thyroid medication interactions require specialist input

subgroups.name: Thyroid nodules and non-autoimmune dysfunction
subgroups.description: Thyroid nodules are extremely common and usually benign. Toxic nodules or multinodular goitre can cause hyperthyroidism independent of autoimmune mechanisms.
distinguishing_features: Single or multiple nodules detectable on ultrasound or palpation
Toxic nodules produce excess hormone autonomously (not antibody-driven)
Fine needle aspiration biopsy may be required to exclude malignancy
Iodine deficiency is a key driver in some geographic regions
Radioiodine ablation or surgery may be required

symptoms.symptom: Persistent fatigue and low energy not relieved by sleep
symptoms.notes: Often described as a 'heavy' or 'cellular' tiredness distinct from ordinary tiredness. One of the most common and debilitating symptoms of hypothyroidism.

symptoms.symptom: Unexplained weight gain or inability to lose weight
symptoms.notes: Driven by slowed metabolic rate. May persist even with calorie restriction and exercise in hypothyroidism.
More characteristic of hyperthyroidism — appetite may increase while weight drops.

symptoms.symptom: Cold intolerance — feeling cold when others are comfortable
symptoms.notes: A hallmark of hypothyroidism. Cold hands and feet are especially common.
More characteristic of hyperthyroidism.

symptoms.symptom: Brain fog — difficulty concentrating, slow thinking, poor memory
symptoms.notes: Can be profound in hypothyroidism. Often dismissed as depression or anxiety. May reflect poor T3 delivery to brain tissue.
Thyroid hormone is required for serotonin and dopamine synthesis. Hypothyroidism-related depression may not respond adequately to antidepressants alone.
More prominent in hyperthyroidism but can also occur in Hashimoto's during inflammatory flares.
Skin may feel rough, scaly, or prematurely aged. Hair often becomes coarser.
Can affect the scalp and body hair. Outer third eyebrow thinning is classically associated with hypothyroidism (though non-specific).
Hypothyroidism slows gut motility throughout the GI tract. Can mimic or worsen IBS-C.
More characteristic of hyperthyroidism. Accelerated gut transit.
A prominent feature of hyperthyroidism. Can also occur during Hashimoto's flares.
Associated with hypothyroidism. May cause dizziness, low exercise tolerance, and shortness of breath.
Hypothyroidism impairs cholesterol clearance. Persistently high LDL or total cholesterol that does not respond to diet may warrant thyroid investigation.
Hypothyroidism reduces energy availability to muscle tissue. CK (creatine kinase) may be elevated.
Can mimic inflammatory arthritis. Often worse in the morning in hypothyroidism.
Caused by accumulation of glycosaminoglycans in connective tissue. Distinct from fluid retention — does not pit on pressure.
May be visible or palpable at the front of the neck. Present in both Hashimoto's and Graves' disease, and with iodine deficiency.
Hypothyroidism disrupts the HPG axis, leading to menstrual irregularity, heavy bleeding, or amenorrhoea.
Adequate thyroid hormone is critical for ovulation, implantation, and early pregnancy. Many fertility clinics now check TSH and thyroid antibodies routinely.
Common in both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism, through different hormonal mechanisms.
Hyperthyroidism commonly causes insomnia; hypothyroidism may cause hypersomnia or non-restorative sleep.
An enlarged thyroid or myxoedema of the vocal cords can cause these symptoms.
Less commonly recognised but associated with hypothyroidism, particularly in Hashimoto's.
Myxoedematous tissue changes can compress the median nerve. Should prompt thyroid investigation when bilateral or unexplained.

key_patterns: TSH elevated above 2.5–3.0 mIU/L (some functional practitioners use a tighter range of 1.0–2.0) combined with symptoms warrants further investigation
Positive anti-TPO or anti-thyroglobulin antibodies confirm autoimmune thyroid disease even when TSH is still within range
Low free T3 with normal or high free T4 suggests impaired T4-to-T3 conversion
Elevated reverse T3 (rT3) in the context of low free T3 suggests functional T3 deficiency even when TSH appears normal
Elevated LDL cholesterol unexplained by diet or lifestyle — should prompt thyroid testing
Low ferritin, B12, vitamin D, magnesium, or selenium alongside thyroid symptoms — these deficiencies impair thyroid function and conversion

at_home.name: Basal body temperature tracking (Barnes method)
at_home.description: Some practitioners use underarm temperature taken immediately on waking (before getting up) as a proxy for metabolic rate. A consistently low resting temperature (below approximately 36.3°C / 97.3°F) may suggest low thyroid function. This is not diagnostic but can support clinical suspicion and provide useful data to share with a practitioner.
at_home.safety_notes: This is a supportive observation tool only. Results are affected by many factors including infection, menstrual cycle phase, and sleep quality. Women should track relative to their cycle. Do not use this alone to self-diagnose or self-treat.

at_home.name: Symptom diary
at_home.description: Tracking symptom severity, timing, energy levels, temperature sensitivity, sleep quality, hair and skin changes, and bowel patterns over 4–8 weeks can reveal patterns that support a clinical case for investigation — particularly when standard tests return 'normal' results.
at_home.safety_notes: Useful for demonstrating symptom burden and patterns to clinicians who may otherwise dismiss complaints.

at_home.name: Dry skin / hair texture self-assessment
at_home.description: Regular attention to changes in skin moisture, hair texture, eyebrow fullness, and nail brittleness can document physical markers of hypothyroidism that emerge gradually and are easy to overlook.
at_home.safety_notes: These changes are non-specific and have many potential causes. Use as part of a broader picture.

clinical.name: TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone)
clinical.description: The most commonly ordered first-line test. Measures pituitary output, which rises when thyroid hormone is insufficient. Sensitive but not sufficient alone — it reflects pituitary response, not actual tissue hormone levels. Many symptomatic individuals fall within the 'normal' range (typically 0.4–4.0 mIU/L) but may benefit from investigation with additional markers.
clinical.availability: Ordered by any GP or primary care physician. Also available through private/direct-access blood testing services.

clinical.name: Free T4 (fT4)
clinical.description: Measures the unbound, biologically active form of thyroxine. Should be ordered alongside TSH for a more complete picture. Low fT4 with elevated TSH confirms overt hypothyroidism.
clinical.availability: Routinely available via GP referral; often included in standard thyroid panels.

clinical.name: Free T3 (fT3)
clinical.description: Measures the active form of thyroid hormone that enters cells. Critical for identifying poor conversion from T4 to T3. Frequently omitted from standard NHS/GP panels in the UK and many US insurance panels. Low fT3 despite normal TSH is a key finding in conversion dysfunction.
clinical.availability: Often requires private testing or an endocrinologist/functional medicine referral. Available through private labs (e.g. Medichecks, Thriva in the UK; LabCorp, Quest in the US).

clinical.name: Thyroid antibodies (anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin)
clinical.description: Anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) and anti-thyroglobulin (anti-TG) antibodies confirm autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's). Can be positive years before TSH becomes abnormal. Important for prognosis and explaining symptom fluctuation.
clinical.availability: Available via GP in many countries when autoimmune disease is suspected; often included in comprehensive private thyroid panels.

clinical.name: TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb / TSI)
clinical.description: Confirms Graves' disease (autoimmune hyperthyroidism). Distinguishes Graves' from other causes of hyperthyroidism.
clinical.availability: Ordered by endocrinologists or when hyperthyroidism is being investigated.

clinical.name: Reverse T3 (rT3)
clinical.description: T4 can be converted to inactive reverse T3 rather than active T3, particularly under physiological stress, illness, or calorie restriction. An elevated rT3 relative to free T3 may indicate functional T3 deficiency. This test is not widely accepted in conventional endocrinology but is used in functional medicine to investigate treatment-resistant symptoms.
clinical.availability: Not routinely available on NHS; available through private labs. Interpretation is specialist-dependent.

clinical.name: Thyroid ultrasound
clinical.description: Visualises gland structure, size, and texture. Identifies heterogeneous or hypoechoic tissue (consistent with Hashimoto's), nodules, goitre, and vascularisation changes in Graves' disease. Can identify structural causes of thyroid dysfunction independent of blood markers.
clinical.availability: Ordered by GPs, endocrinologists, or radiologists. Widely available in public and private healthcare.

clinical.name: Radioiodine uptake scan
clinical.description: Used to differentiate causes of hyperthyroidism — Graves' disease shows diffuse uptake, toxic nodular goitre shows focal uptake, and thyroiditis typically shows very low uptake. Not used in hypothyroidism investigation.
clinical.availability: Available at nuclear medicine departments in hospitals. Requires specialist referral.

clinical.name: Comprehensive thyroid panel (private)
clinical.description: Includes TSH, free T4, free T3, reverse T3, anti-TPO, and anti-TG in a single blood draw. The most complete picture of thyroid function and immune status. Essential for identifying conversion problems and autoimmune disease that standard NHS/insurance panels may miss.
clinical.availability: Available through private laboratories such as Medichecks (UK), Thriva (UK), Ulta Lab Tests (US), or via functional medicine practitioners.

clinical.name: Nutrient panel relevant to thyroid function
clinical.description: Selenium, iodine (urinary), ferritin (not just haemoglobin), zinc, magnesium, and vitamin D all play direct roles in thyroid hormone synthesis and T4-to-T3 conversion. Deficiencies are common in thyroid patients and can prevent recovery despite medication.
clinical.availability: Some markers available via GP; more comprehensive panels through private labs or integrative practitioners.

clinical.name: Fine needle aspiration biopsy (FNAB)
clinical.description: Used when thyroid nodules are identified on ultrasound and malignancy cannot be excluded by imaging alone. A thin needle removes cells from the nodule for cytological examination.
clinical.availability: Performed by endocrinologists, radiologists, or surgeons at specialist centres.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Autoimmune thyroid disease (especially Hashimoto's) is significantly more common in people with coeliac disease than in the general population. Undiagnosed coeliac disease can also impair absorption of thyroid medication (levothyroxine). Testing for coeliac disease is recommended when Hashimoto's is present.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Hypothyroidism slows gut motility and can cause bloating, constipation, and abdominal discomfort that closely mimics IBS-C. Hyperthyroidism can cause diarrhoea that mimics IBS-D. Thyroid dysfunction should be excluded before an IBS diagnosis is accepted.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: Hypothyroidism significantly slows intestinal motility, including the migrating motor complex (MMC) that clears bacteria between meals. This creates conditions for SIBO to develop. SIBO in turn can impair absorption of levothyroxine and nutrients critical for thyroid function.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Autoimmune thyroid disease is associated with mast cell dysregulation. Thyroid autoantibodies may directly activate mast cells, and some people with Hashimoto's report significant histamine and mast cell-type reactions that improve with thyroid optimisation.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Thyroid hormone plays a role in regulating DAO (diamine oxidase), the enzyme that breaks down histamine. Hypothyroidism may impair histamine clearance, contributing to food-related histamine reactions in some individuals.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism can cause autonomic nervous system symptoms including palpitations, dizziness, and exercise intolerance that resemble POTS. Thyroid dysfunction can also trigger or worsen true dysautonomia.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism and Hashimoto's flares can cause anxiety, racing thoughts, palpitations, and hyperarousal that are clinically indistinguishable from generalised anxiety disorder. Hypothyroidism can cause depression that presents with anxious features. Thyroid status should always be checked before psychiatric diagnoses are made.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Hypothyroidism is one of the most important reversible causes of depression. Thyroid hormones are required for neurotransmitter synthesis and receptor sensitivity. Many people with hypothyroid depression are treated with antidepressants for years before the underlying thyroid cause is identified.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: The muscle pain, fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and sleep disturbance of hypothyroidism closely overlap with fibromyalgia. Some researchers propose that a subset of fibromyalgia represents unrecognised or inadequately treated thyroid dysfunction.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Autoimmune thyroid disease is more prevalent in people with endometriosis than in the general population. Both conditions involve immune dysregulation. Thyroid dysfunction worsens menstrual irregularity and pain in those with both conditions.

related_conditions.name: Hormonal / menstrual cycle effects
related_conditions.explanation: Thyroid hormones are deeply intertwined with sex hormone regulation. Hypothyroidism disrupts ovulation, progesterone production, and the HPG axis, leading to cycle irregularities, PMS, and heavy bleeding. Oestrogen also affects thyroid hormone binding and availability.

related_conditions.name: ADHD
related_conditions.explanation: The brain fog, poor concentration, distractibility, and impulsivity that can occur with thyroid dysfunction — particularly Hashimoto's and poor T3 conversion — can resemble ADHD in adults. Thyroid function should be assessed before or alongside ADHD evaluation.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: The gut microbiome plays a role in the conversion of T4 to T3 and in the deconjugation of thyroid hormones from bile for reabsorption. Dysbiosis may impair these processes. Leaky gut associated with dysbiosis may also drive the autoimmune response in Hashimoto's and Graves' disease.

related_conditions.name: Adrenal dysfunction (HPA axis dysregulation)
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic stress elevates cortisol, which suppresses TSH production, impairs T4-to-T3 conversion, and promotes reverse T3 formation. Many people with thyroid dysfunction also have dysregulated cortisol patterns. Treating thyroid alone without addressing adrenal function often produces incomplete recovery.

related_conditions.name: Type 1 diabetes
related_conditions.explanation: Both are autoimmune endocrine conditions. People with Type 1 diabetes have significantly elevated rates of autoimmune thyroid disease, and vice versa. Shared immune regulatory pathways are involved.

related_conditions.name: Rheumatoid arthritis
related_conditions.explanation: Autoimmune thyroid disease co-occurs with rheumatoid arthritis at higher than expected rates. Both involve chronic immune activation, and the joint pain and fatigue of hypothyroidism can overlap considerably with RA symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Vitiligo
related_conditions.explanation: Vitiligo is an autoimmune skin condition that clusters with other autoimmune conditions, particularly Hashimoto's and Graves' disease, reflecting shared immune dysregulation.

related_conditions.name: Alopecia areata
related_conditions.explanation: Autoimmune hair loss (alopecia areata) co-occurs with Hashimoto's and Graves' disease more commonly than chance would predict. Thyroid function should be assessed in people presenting with unexplained hair loss.

related_conditions.name: Infertility
related_conditions.explanation: Hypothyroidism, particularly when accompanied by elevated TSH or thyroid antibodies, significantly impairs fertility through disrupted ovulation, poor endometrial receptivity, and early miscarriage. Optimal thyroid function (TSH below 2.5 when trying to conceive) is now recommended by many fertility specialists.

related_conditions.name: Cardiovascular disease
related_conditions.explanation: Both overt and subclinical hypothyroidism are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol, impaired endothelial function, and increased cardiovascular risk. Hyperthyroidism increases atrial fibrillation risk. Treating thyroid dysfunction reduces some of these risks.

strategies.name: Levothyroxine (LT4) — standard T4 replacement
strategies.description: Synthetic T4 is the first-line treatment for hypothyroidism in most countries. It requires conversion to active T3 in peripheral tissues. Works well for many people but inadequately addresses symptoms in those with poor conversion capacity. Dose should be adjusted based on symptoms AND blood markers, not TSH alone.
strategies.notes: Taken on an empty stomach, 30–60 minutes before food. Coffee, calcium, iron supplements, and many medications impair absorption. Consistent timing and brand matter — bioavailability varies between manufacturers.

strategies.name: Combination T4/T3 therapy (LT4 + liothyronine)
strategies.description: Adding small doses of liothyronine (synthetic T3) to levothyroxine may benefit individuals who continue to experience symptoms — particularly brain fog and fatigue — despite optimal T4 dosing. Some randomised trials show mood and quality-of-life benefits, particularly in those with DIO2 gene variants impairing conversion.
strategies.notes: Remains controversial in mainstream endocrinology. Short T3 half-life requires careful dosing to avoid peaks and troughs. Should be managed by a practitioner comfortable with combination therapy — not all endocrinologists will prescribe it.

strategies.name: Natural desiccated thyroid (NDT — e.g. Armour Thyroid, NP Thyroid)
strategies.description: Derived from porcine thyroid gland and contains both T4 and T3 (and small amounts of T2, T1, and calcitonin) in a 4:1 ratio. Some individuals report superior symptom resolution compared with levothyroxine alone. Preferred by some functional medicine practitioners for poor converters.
strategies.notes: Not universally available or prescribable in all countries. Contains a fixed T4:T3 ratio that may not suit everyone. T3 content can cause palpitations or anxiety if dose is too high. Some practitioners are strongly opposed; finding one open to NDT may require research.

strategies.name: Gluten-free diet (for Hashimoto's)
strategies.description: Several studies and significant clinical observation suggest that a strict gluten-free diet may reduce anti-TPO antibody levels and inflammation in people with Hashimoto's, particularly those with coeliac disease or non-coeliac gluten sensitivity. The mechanism may involve molecular mimicry between gliadin and thyroid tissue antigens, and gut-immune axis effects.
strategies.notes: Evidence is stronger in those who also have coeliac disease or elevated gliadin antibodies. A 3–6 month strict trial is typically needed to assess response. Not all patients with Hashimoto's will benefit. Coeliac disease should be tested (while still eating gluten) before initiating the diet.

strategies.name: Selenium supplementation
strategies.description: Selenium is essential for the deiodinase enzymes that convert T4 to T3 and for the antioxidant enzyme glutathione peroxidase, which protects thyroid tissue. Supplementation at 200 mcg/day of selenomethionine has been shown in multiple trials to reduce anti-TPO antibody levels in Hashimoto's and improve thyroid function markers.
strategies.notes: Benefits are most consistent in selenium-deficient populations. Excess selenium is toxic — do not exceed 400 mcg/day from all sources. Brazil nuts contain variable but high selenium and can substitute for supplements in some cases. Test serum selenium before supplementing if possible.

strategies.name: Iron / ferritin optimisation
strategies.description: Iron is required for thyroid peroxidase (the enzyme responsible for producing thyroid hormones) and for T4-to-T3 conversion. Low ferritin (often below 70–100 ng/mL is functionally relevant for thyroid patients, even if technically 'within range') is extremely common and impairs response to thyroid treatment.
strategies.notes: Ferritin, not just haemoglobin, should be measured. Iron supplements should not be taken within 4 hours of thyroid medication as they impair absorption. Food sources include red meat, organ meats, and legumes with vitamin C.

strategies.name: Vitamin D optimisation
strategies.description: Vitamin D deficiency is strongly associated with autoimmune thyroid disease. Vitamin D receptors are present on immune cells and thyroid tissue. Optimising levels (typically to 100–150 nmol/L or 40–60 ng/mL) may help modulate the autoimmune response in Hashimoto's and Graves' disease.
strategies.notes: Should be tested before supplementing. High-dose supplementation requires K2 co-administration to manage calcium metabolism. Best achieved through a combination of sensible sun exposure and supplementation where deficient.

strategies.name: Stress reduction and HPA axis support
strategies.description: Chronic psychological and physiological stress elevates cortisol, suppresses TSH, promotes reverse T3, and worsens immune dysregulation. Addressing the HPA axis through consistent sleep, stress management practices, and reduction of physiological stressors (overtraining, calorie restriction) is a foundational part of thyroid recovery.
strategies.notes: Often overlooked in purely medicated approaches. HPA axis support may include gentle exercise, sleep hygiene, adaptogenic herbs (e.g. ashwagandha — which also has direct thyroid-supportive effects), and psychological support.

strategies.name: Low-inflammatory or autoimmune protocol (AIP) diet
strategies.description: The Autoimmune Protocol (AIP) is an elimination diet that removes potential immune triggers (grains, legumes, nightshades, dairy, eggs, nuts, seeds) to reduce systemic inflammation and gut permeability. Some individuals with Hashimoto's report significant symptom and antibody improvement. Typically used as a 30–90 day elimination and reintroduction protocol.
strategies.notes: One small pilot study showed improvements in quality of life and inflammatory markers in Hashimoto's patients on AIP. Requires careful nutritional planning to avoid deficiencies. Best supported by a registered dietitian familiar with autoimmune conditions.

strategies.name: Adequate iodine (neither too little nor too much)
strategies.description: Iodine is required for thyroid hormone synthesis, but excess iodine can trigger or worsen autoimmune thyroid disease (the Wolff-Chaikoff effect). Both deficiency and excess are problematic for Hashimoto's patients particularly.
strategies.notes: High-dose iodine supplementation marketed for thyroid health is potentially dangerous for people with autoimmune thyroid disease. Dietary iodine from food is generally safe. Those considering iodine supplementation should do so under practitioner supervision and only if deficiency is confirmed.

strategies.name: Optimising levothyroxine absorption
strategies.description: Consistent timing (30–60 minutes before any food or drink except water), avoiding interfering substances (coffee, calcium, iron, antacids), and consistent brand use can significantly affect how much T4 reaches the bloodstream. Many people on stable medication doses experience worsening of symptoms due to absorption issues without realising.
strategies.notes: Switching formulation (e.g. from tablet to liquid levothyroxine) can help people with absorption issues related to gut conditions or achlorhydria.

things_to_avoid: High-dose iodine supplements — can trigger or dramatically worsen autoimmune thyroid flares, especially in Hashimoto's patients
Taking thyroid medication alongside calcium, iron, antacids, or proton pump inhibitors — significantly reduces absorption
Coffee within 60 minutes of levothyroxine — impairs absorption even when taken with water
Extreme calorie restriction or prolonged fasting — suppresses T3, elevates reverse T3, and worsens functional hypothyroid symptoms
Overtraining without adequate recovery — acts as a physiological stressor that drives rT3 formation and suppresses thyroid function
Accepting a 'normal TSH' as the complete answer when symptoms persist — TSH alone is an incomplete measure of thyroid status at the cellular level
Abruptly stopping thyroid medication without medical supervision — can precipitate myxoedema crisis in severely hypothyroid individuals

Excessive raw cruciferous vegetables if severely iodine-deficient — contain goitrogenic compounds that may mildly inhibit thyroid function; cooking largely neutralises this effect and quantities typically consumed are not a concern for most people
Assuming antidepressants are the correct treatment for low mood if thyroid status has not been fully assessed
Supplementing with high-dose biotin before thyroid blood tests — biotin interferes with immunoassay testing and can produce falsely abnormal TSH and thyroid hormone results

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Standard TSH testing alone misses a significant number of people with functionally inadequate thyroid hormone at the cellular level. Receiving a 'normal' result does not mean the thyroid system is functioning optimally.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People trust that standard tests catch everything, and doctors are often confident in the TSH reference range. Questioning a 'normal' result feels like challenging medical authority or being a hypochondriac.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who pursue full thyroid panels — including free T3, reverse T3, and antibodies — through private testing or functional medicine practitioners often identify specific deficits that explain persistent symptoms and enable targeted treatment.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Levothyroxine is not the right treatment for everyone with hypothyroidism. A meaningful subset of patients continue to have significant symptoms on T4-only therapy due to poor conversion, and will not fully recover without T3 supplementation or desiccated thyroid.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Mainstream endocrinology has largely dismissed combination therapy based on early inconclusive trials. Patients are frequently told their symptoms are due to other causes once TSH normalises, and many accept this explanation rather than advocating for further investigation.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who identify poor T3 conversion through testing and find a practitioner willing to trial combination therapy or NDT often report substantial improvements in energy, cognition, and quality of life that they had not experienced on levothyroxine alone.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's) is driven by an immune dysfunction, not just a hormone deficiency. Replacing the missing hormone without addressing immune dysregulation leaves the underlying driver unaddressed.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Standard care for Hashimoto's is purely hormonal replacement. The idea that diet, gut health, stress, and infection might be upstream drivers is not part of conventional thyroid management, and many patients are not told that the autoimmune process continues to damage their gland.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People with Hashimoto's who investigate gut health, eliminate coeliac/gluten sensitivity, optimise nutritional deficiencies, and reduce inflammatory load often experience measurable reductions in antibody levels and symptom burden over 6–18 months.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Thyroid conditions are chronic. There is often no quick fix, and recovery may take 1–2 years of consistent, systematic effort across multiple levers (medication, nutrition, gut health, stress, sleep).
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The desire for a single explanation and a single treatment is powerful. Many patients cycle through different practitioners and treatments looking for the one solution, rather than committing to sustained multifactorial management.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who track a small number of relevant markers over time, work systematically through known drivers, and build a consistent daily framework — rather than seeking single solutions — tend to achieve stable, meaningful improvement.

key_levers.lever: Achieve optimal thyroid hormone levels — not just 'normal'
key_levers.why_it_matters: TSH within the reference range does not guarantee adequate T3 at the cellular level. Optimising free T3 (and suppressing reverse T3) is the target for true functional restoration. Many people remain significantly symptomatic with TSH in the upper half of the reference range.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a comprehensive thyroid panel including free T3 and reverse T3. If these have never been tested, this is the logical first investigation.
key_levers.timeline: Weeks to months to identify the right medication type and dose; stability typically requires 6–12 months of optimisation.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Selenium, ferritin, vitamin D, zinc, magnesium, and vitamin B12 are all required for thyroid hormone synthesis, conversion, and receptor function. Deficiencies are common in thyroid patients and can prevent full recovery even on optimal medication.
key_levers.how_to_start: Test ferritin, vitamin D, selenium, B12, and magnesium. Target ferritin above 70–100 ng/mL, vitamin D at 100–150 nmol/L, and selenium at the mid-upper reference range.
key_levers.timeline: 3–6 months to meaningfully correct most deficiencies with targeted supplementation.

key_levers.why_it_matters: The gut is critical to thyroid function — for T4-to-T3 conversion, for levothyroxine absorption, and for immune regulation driving autoimmunity. SIBO, dysbiosis, coeliac disease, and leaky gut all impair thyroid recovery.
key_levers.how_to_start: Test for coeliac disease (if still eating gluten). Consider a trial elimination of gluten and assess response over 3 months. Investigate SIBO if constipation, bloating, or IBS-type symptoms are present.
key_levers.timeline: 3–12 months to meaningfully improve gut function and observe thyroid-related benefits.

key_levers.why_it_matters: In autoimmune thyroid disease, the underlying process is immune-driven. Chronic infections, food antigens, gut permeability, stress, and environmental toxins all sustain immune activation. Reducing these inputs lowers antibody production and tissue damage over time.
key_levers.how_to_start: Identify the most likely immune stressor for the individual — common starting points include gluten elimination, H. pylori testing, and stress reduction practices.
key_levers.timeline: 6–18 months to observe meaningful antibody reduction.

key_levers.lever: Stabilise the HPA axis and reduce physiological stress
key_levers.why_it_matters: Cortisol directly suppresses TSH and promotes conversion of T4 to inactive reverse T3 rather than active T3. An overactivated stress system will continuously undermine thyroid function regardless of medication dose.
key_levers.how_to_start: Prioritise 7–9 hours of consistent sleep. Identify major physiological stressors (overtraining, undereating, irregular sleep). Consider morning cortisol or 4-point cortisol testing to assess HPA axis function.
key_levers.timeline: 3–6 months of consistent lifestyle change to see meaningful HPA axis improvement.

key_levers.lever: Find a practitioner who investigates, not just manages
key_levers.why_it_matters: Standard thyroid care focuses on normalising TSH with levothyroxine. Many of the most impactful interventions — combination therapy, conversion investigation, antibody-lowering strategies — require a practitioner who looks beyond the standard protocol.
key_levers.how_to_start: Seek a functional medicine doctor, integrative endocrinologist, or thyroid-specialist GP who uses full thyroid panels, considers conversion issues, and is open to multiple treatment approaches.

common_mistakes: Accepting 'your TSH is normal' as the final word when symptoms persist — this forecloses investigation of free T3, reverse T3, and antibodies that may explain ongoing problems
Starting multiple interventions simultaneously — making it impossible to identify what is actually helping
Discontinuing thyroid medication when feeling better — in most cases of Hashimoto's, medication is lifelong and symptoms will return if stopped
Expecting rapid results — thyroid hormone changes operate on a timescale of weeks to months, and most meaningful improvements take 6–12 months of consistent management
Over-supplementing iodine — often marketed as beneficial for thyroid health, high-dose iodine can trigger autoimmune flares
Focusing exclusively on medication while ignoring diet, gut, stress, and nutritional deficiencies — all of which can prevent full recovery
Treating the lab number rather than the person — optimising TSH to mid-range while the patient remains disabled by fatigue and brain fog is not successful treatment
Not accounting for biotin supplements before thyroid blood tests — leads to falsely reassuring or alarming results

specialists.role: Specialist in hormonal conditions including thyroid disease. Manages complex cases, prescribes radioiodine, and oversees thyroid cancer follow-up. First-line specialist referral for hyperthyroidism, thyroid nodules, and treatment-resistant hypothyroidism.
specialists.notes: Many endocrinologists follow strict TSH-based protocols and are reluctant to prescribe combination T4/T3 therapy or investigate conversion issues. Finding an endocrinologist open to broader investigation and treatment options may require effort. Academic or integrative endocrinologists tend to have more flexibility.

specialists.role: Investigates root causes of thyroid dysfunction including gut health, nutrient deficiencies, HPA axis dysregulation, and immune drivers. Typically uses comprehensive thyroid panels and is open to combination therapy and NDT.
specialists.notes: Not all functional medicine practitioners are equally rigorous. Look for practitioners with formal functional medicine training (e.g. IFM-certified) and experience specifically with thyroid conditions. Often practise privately.

specialists.type: Integrative or holistic GP / primary care physician
specialists.role: A GP or internist with training in integrative or nutritional medicine who can manage thyroid conditions with a broader lens — including addressing nutritional deficiencies, lifestyle factors, and autoimmune drivers alongside medication.
specialists.notes: Increasingly available but still a minority within primary care. May be found through integrative medicine directories or patient community recommendations.

specialists.type: Registered dietitian (with autoimmune or thyroid experience)
specialists.role: Provides evidence-based nutritional support for managing Hashimoto's and Graves' disease — including gluten-free and AIP dietary protocols, managing nutritional deficiencies, and supporting gut health restoration.
specialists.notes: Seek a dietitian with specific experience in autoimmune conditions. General dietitians may not be familiar with the dietary nuances relevant to thyroid autoimmunity.

specialists.role: Critical for individuals with thyroid dysfunction and fertility difficulties or recurrent miscarriage. Manages thyroid optimisation during pre-conception and pregnancy, where targets are tighter (TSH below 2.5).
specialists.notes: Thyroid status should be optimised before any fertility treatment. Anti-TPO antibodies are an independent risk factor for miscarriage even with normal TSH.

patient_communities.name: r/Hypothyroidism
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community of people managing hypothyroidism. Useful for sharing experiences with medications, practitioners, and testing. Pinned resources include guides to optimal testing and finding helpful practitioners.

patient_communities.name: r/Hashimotos
patient_communities.notes: Focused specifically on Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Good source of experience-sharing around dietary interventions, antibody reduction, and managing the autoimmune component alongside standard care.

patient_communities.name: Thyroid UK
patient_communities.notes: UK-based patient organisation with extensive evidence-based resources, a practitioner directory, and a HealthUnlocked forum community. Particularly useful for UK patients navigating NHS limitations and finding practitioners open to combination therapy.

patient_communities.name: HealthUnlocked — Thyroid UK community
patient_communities.notes: One of the most active and knowledgeable thyroid patient communities online. Members include highly experienced self-advocates who can help interpret test results and navigate treatment options. Moderated for evidence quality.

patient_communities.name: Stop the Thyroid Madness community
patient_communities.notes: Advocacy resource focused on the limitations of T4-only treatment and the case for optimal free T3, combination therapy, and desiccated thyroid. Strong bias toward NDT; should be balanced with clinical guidance, but useful for understanding the treatment landscape.

patient_communities.name: Graves' Disease & Thyroid Foundation
patient_communities.notes: Focused on Graves' disease and hyperthyroidism. Provides patient education on treatment options (medication, radioiodine, surgery) and community support for those navigating a Graves' diagnosis.

learning_resources.title: Why Do I Still Have Thyroid Symptoms When My Lab Tests Are Normal?
learning_resources.description: A foundational text exploring the autoimmune and conversion dimensions of thyroid dysfunction that fall outside standard medical management. Written by a functional medicine clinician and academic. Particularly useful for those with Hashimoto's who continue to suffer despite medication.

learning_resources.title: The Thyroid Connection
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive guide to understanding thyroid dysfunction from a functional medicine perspective, covering diet, gut health, stress, toxins, and infections as upstream drivers. Accessible and practical for self-directed investigation.

learning_resources.title: Thyroid Pharmacology
learning_resources.description: A rigorous review of thyroid hormone metabolism, deiodinase enzyme function, and the clinical implications of T4-to-T3 conversion — the scientific basis for understanding why T4-only treatment may be insufficient for some patients.

learning_resources.title: Stop the Thyroid Madness (website)
learning_resources.description: An extensive patient-built resource covering the limitations of TSH testing, optimal thyroid hormone levels, desiccated thyroid treatment, adrenal connections, and navigating the medical system. Carries a strong NDT advocacy position; read alongside mainstream clinical guidance.

learning_resources.title: Thyroid UK — Evidence and Resources
learning_resources.description: Well-curated patient organisation website with summaries of research on combination therapy, optimal TSH ranges, and the clinical case for broader testing. A good starting point for UK patients wanting to understand their options within and beyond NHS care.

learning_resources.title: The Role of Selenium in Thyroid Autoimmunity and Cancer
learning_resources.description: A systematic review of the evidence for selenium supplementation in autoimmune thyroid disease, with particular focus on anti-TPO antibody reduction in Hashimoto's. Useful for those wishing to understand the evidence base before supplementing.

learning_resources.title: Izabella Wentz — Thyroid Pharmacist
learning_resources.description: A pharmacy-trained clinician who developed Hashimoto's herself and has built a substantial evidence-based resource around root-cause investigation — particularly gut health, infections, nutrient deficiencies, and lifestyle factors in autoimmune thyroid disease.

learning_resources.title: Hashimoto's Protocol
learning_resources.description: A structured 90-day programme addressing the root causes of Hashimoto's through diet, gut health, stress, toxin reduction, and infection. Practical and well-referenced. Best suited to those already diagnosed with Hashimoto's who want to move beyond medication-only management.

learning_resources.title: ATA Clinical Practice Guidelines for Hypothyroidism
learning_resources.description: The mainstream clinical guideline for hypothyroidism management. Useful for understanding what the standard of care is — and where its limitations lie relative to patient experience. A reference point for informed conversations with clinicians.

sections.relevance_signal: **Constipation with fatigue and weight gain** (hypothyroid) or **diarrhoea with weight loss and anxiety** (hyperthyroid). Gut symptoms are part of a broader systemic picture.
